name: Eswatini
excerpt: A Swazi kingdom was founded in the mid-18th century and ruled by a series of kings, including MSWATI II, a 19th century ruler whose name was adopted for the country and its predominant ethnic group.

quick_facts.capital: Mbabane (administrative capital); Lobamba (royal and legislative capital)
quick_facts.languages: English (official, used for government business), siSwati (official)
quick_facts.religions: Christian 90% (Zionist - a blend of Christianity and traditional African religions - 40%, Roman Catholic 20%, other Christian 30% - includes Anglican, Methodist, Church of Jesus Christ, Jehovah's Witness), Muslim 2%, other 8% (includes Baha'i, Buddhist, Hindu, indigenous, Jewish) (2015 est.)
quick_facts.ethnic_groups: predominantly Swazi; smaller populations of other African ethnic groups, including the Zulu, as well as people of European ancestry
quick_facts.climate: varies from tropical to near temperate
quick_facts.terrain: mostly mountains and hills; some moderately sloping plains
quick_facts.natural_resources: asbestos, coal, clay, cassiterite, hydropower, forests, small gold and diamond deposits, quarry stone, and talc
quick_facts.major_exports: scented mixtures, raw sugar, industrial acids/oils/alcohols, garments, wood (2023)

summaries.geography_summary: landlocked; almost completely surrounded by South Africa
summaries.history_summary: A Swazi kingdom was founded in the mid-18th century and ruled by a series of kings, including MSWATI II, a 19th century ruler whose name was adopted for the country and its predominant ethnic group. European countries defined the kingdom’s modern borders during the late-19th century, and Swaziland (as it became known) was administered as a UK high commission territory from 1903 until its independence in 1968. A new constitution that came into effect in 2005 included provisions for a more independent parliament and judiciary, but the legal status of political parties remains unclear, and the kingdom is still considered an absolute monarchy. King MSWATI III renamed the country from Swaziland to Eswatini in 2018 to reflect the name most commonly used by its citizens. In 2021, MSWATI III used security forces to suppress prodemocracy protests. A national dialogue and reconciliation process agreed to in the wake of violence has not materialized. In November 2023, King MSWATI III appointed a new prime minister following peaceful national elections. Despite its classification as a lower-middle income country, Eswatini suffers from severe poverty, corruption, and high unemployment. Eswatini has the world's highest HIV/AIDS prevalence rate, although recent years have shown marked declines in new infections. Eswatini is the only country in Africa that recognizes Taiwan.
summaries.government_summary: absolute monarchy. Independence: 6 September 1968 (from the UK)
summaries.economy_summary: landlocked southern African economy; South African trade dependent and currency pegging; CMA and SACU member state; COVID-19 economic slowdown; growing utilities inflation; persistent poverty and unemployment; HIV/AIDS labor force disruptions

suggested_reading: CIA World Factbook data (archived via factbook.json) | https://github.com/factbook/factbook.json

prayer_and_mission.prayer_intro: Eswatini has a large Christian majority, though much of it blends Christian identity with traditional Zionist and African indigenous religious practice. Pray for genuine, biblically grounded faith to take root distinct from syncretistic blending.
prayer_points: Pray for Eswatini's Christian churches to be equipped with sound, Scripture-centered teaching.
Pray for those practicing syncretistic Zionist and traditional religion to hear and receive the clear biblical gospel.
Pray for pastors to be trained in expository preaching.
prayer_and_mission.mission_emphasis: Eswatini's Christian majority often blends biblical faith with traditional African religious practice, leaving significant need for clear biblical teaching. Ongoing needs include training national pastors in sound doctrine and reaching syncretistic movements with the unmixed biblical gospel.
